purpose: Determines whether a wage-price spiral is likely to emerge by requiring all three necessary conditions to hold simultaneously
components: Unemployment below 3.8%
Wage growth exceeding 5%
Inflation expectations de-anchored
current_assessment: 2/3 conditions were met during the 2021-2023 stress test (UE 3.4%, wages ~6.7%) without spiral materializing. Expectations remained anchored. Currently no conditions met. Institutional suppression (union density ~10%, COLA coverage <2%) provides structural protection.
limitations: Small sample: only 8 spiral episodes since 1960 for a conjunctive test
Novel mechanisms (algorithmic pricing, gig contract CPI indexation, social media wage expectations) are untested
The institutional argument depends on union density and COLA coverage remaining low

purpose: Provides a monitorable checklist for tracking labor market transitions from resilience to recession with calibrated traversal times
components: Stage 1: Declining hires, stable layoffs (current position)
Stage 2: Hires stagnation, temp help contraction broadens
Stage 3: Initial claims rising, quits rate declining
Stage 4: Layoffs rising, continuing claims accelerating
Stage 5: Unemployment rate triggering Sahm Rule
Stage 6: Nonlinear unemployment acceleration (hoarding break)
current_assessment: Currently at Stage 1 with declining hires and stable layoffs. Temp help has been negative for 36 months without broader deterioration — either the indicator is structurally broken or fiscal extension is historically unprecedented. Standard traversal: 12-24 months; fiscal-supported: 18-36 months.
limitations: Temp help anomaly (36 months negative) is unresolved — could indicate structural break in the indicator
Fiscal support may extend traversal time beyond historical calibration
Transition signals between stages may be blurred by data quality deterioration
n=1 for the fiscal-extended version

purpose: Meta-framework establishing that downstream analytical precision cannot exceed upstream measurement precision, constraining the entire KB's confidence structure
components: NAIRU 95% CI of ±150bp sets an upper bound on precision of any NAIRU-dependent threshold
NFP ±130K CI limits precision of any employment-level-dependent trigger
Confidence propagation rule: downstream concept confidence ≤ min(upstream confidence × relationship confidence)
KB violates this throughout with downstream concepts at higher confidence than antecedents
current_assessment: The KB specifies margin triggers at 8-9% and ULC thresholds at 3.5% while acknowledging NAIRU uncertainty of ±150bp — a logical contradiction. This is a mathematical constraint, not a debatable interpretation.
limitations: The framework identifies the problem but does not specify how to optimally downgrade dependent concepts
Some thresholds may be empirically robust despite noisy upstream inputs
The framework may be overly conservative if applied mechanically to all causal chains

purpose: Tracks how contradictory labor market assumptions embedded in equity, rate, and credit pricing resolve, with historical base rates for resolution mode and direction
components: Equity signal: S&P 21-22x forward P/E prices ~4.0% UE + robust consumption
Rates signal: 50-75bp easing priced requires labor softening
Credit signal: HY OAS 300-350bp prices no deterioration
Resolution base rates: gradual convergence 67%, sudden repricing 33%, equities 'won' 50%
Median persistence of inconsistency: ~13 months from 6 episodes since 1990
current_assessment: All three asset classes embed contradictory labor assumptions. Immigration reversal removes the supply-side channel that was the only mechanism for reconciling all three simultaneously. Resolution direction is uncertain (equities win 50% of the time).
limitations: Specific probability assignments from asset prices were refuted — can only say 'inconsistent with rapid deterioration'
n=6 for resolution base rates
Each episode had unique features; pattern may not generalize
Resolution can be driven by exogenous shocks unrelated to labor market

purpose: Identifies vulnerability accumulation in dimensions where data opacity is highest, based on the crisis pattern that deterioration consistently originates where opacity meets hidden leverage
components: Data opacity assessment: JOLTS response rate, NFP revision magnitude, birth-death model contribution
Hidden leverage identification: labor hoarding as hidden operating leverage, private credit opacity
Convergence diagnostic: opacity and leverage increasing in same dimension
Monitoring recommendations: HY OAS (500bp concern, 650bp defense), initial claims (260K concern, 300K defense), Indeed postings, cross-currency basis, S&P net margins
current_assessment: JOLTS degradation from 60% to 32% response rate coincides with the period where hoarding dynamics are most policy-relevant. The labor market currently exhibits the fog-of-fragility pattern: deteriorating visibility in the dimension where vulnerability may be accumulating.
limitations: Based on qualitative crisis pattern matching, not quantitative model
Alternative data sources may partially compensate for official data degradation
The framework identifies risk but does not quantify probability
Trigger thresholds (500bp, 260K) inherit measurement-precision concerns

purpose: Systematically scores historical periods against current conditions across 7 dimensions to identify the most relevant analogue for sequencing and timing predictions
components: Fiscal support similarity (current best: 1966-70 at 0.85)
Monetary stance similarity (0.80)
Labor tightness similarity (0.75)
Inflation similarity (0.65)
Wage dynamics similarity (0.60)
Financial structure similarity (0.50)
Labor supply dynamics similarity (0.40)
current_assessment: 1966-70 scores highest at 0.72 overall, but low scores on labor supply (0.40, no immigration shock equivalent) and financial structure (0.50) are gaps in exactly the dimensions that matter most. Forward-looking threshold frameworks may be sounder when analogues are imperfect.
limitations: Low scores in critical dimensions (labor supply 0.40, financial structure 0.50) limit predictive utility
AI + immigration combination has no clean analogue (acknowledged at 9/10 confidence as a limitation)
Unionization 28% then vs 10% now fundamentally weakens wage-price channel comparison
Services share 60% then vs 85% now changes sectoral transmission entirely

purpose: Decomposes aggregate corporate profits using the Kalecki-Levy identity to assess the magnitude and sustainability of fiscal support to the labor market
components: Kalecki-Levy identity: Profits = Investment + Govt Deficit + Net Exports − Household Saving + Dividends
Current fiscal contribution: ~$1.8-2.0T at 6-7% GDP deficit
Counter-example database: Clinton surplus period where profits rose despite fiscal consolidation
Fiscal multiplier range: 0.3-1.5x (Ramey 2019, Blanchard & Leigh 2013)
current_assessment: The identity holds trivially. Large fiscal deficit is currently supporting profits (confidence 7). But the causal claim that withdrawal produces proportional labor weakening is uncertain (confidence 4-5) due to multiplier uncertainty and the Clinton counter-example.
limitations: Identity is always true but causation is uncertain — the framework conflates accounting truth with behavioral mechanism
Fiscal multiplier of 0.3-1.5x is too wide for actionable predictions
1993-2000 counter-example shows private investment can substitute for fiscal withdrawal
Whether current cycle resembles 1993-2000 or 2010-2013 European austerity is a judgment call
